text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Adjuvant Chemotherapy Completion Visit — Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African-descent female with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, MSS, TMB-low), now presenting for follow-up following completion of all 6 planned cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. She is here to review final tumor marker results, discuss her overall clinical status following a highly eventful adjuvant course, and establish a surveillance plan.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Osei-Bonsu's oncologic history is notable for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the head/uncinate process, diagnosed approximately 18 months ago at the time of her initial presentation with epigastric pain, back pain, worsening glycemic control, and significant weight loss. At diagnosis, her CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1,200 U/mL and cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a 3.4 cm pancreatic head mass with SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees and SMV narrowing, categorized as borderline resectable by NCCN criteria.

She received a robust sequence of multimodality treatment:
- Neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX) for 8 cycles, with CA 19-9 declining to 85 U/mL and partial radiographic response (mass decreased to 2.6 cm, SMA abutment reduced to approximately 120 degrees).
- Consolidative long-course chemoradiation with concurrent capecitabine (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions via IMRT), following which CA 19-9 fell further to 52 U/mL and the SMA abutment decreased to approximately 80-90 degrees.
- Pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) performed at a high-volume HPB center. Final pathology: 2.1 cm residual adenocarcinoma, R0 resection, College of American Pathologists treatment response grade 2 (moderate response, ~40% viable tumor), pT3 pN1 M0 (Stage IIB), 2 of 18 lymph nodes positive, lymphovascular and perineural invasion present, SMAD4/DPC4 retained.

The postoperative course was complicated by a grade B pancreatic fistula (ISGPF classification), managed conservatively with NPO, TPN, octreotide, and prolonged drain retention for 6 weeks. She was also noted to have transient delayed gastric emptying. She was ultimately discharged on postoperative day 18.

Adjuvant chemotherapy was initiated with gemcitabine plus capecitabine (ESPAC-4 regimen) approximately 12 weeks after surgery, once the fistula had resolved and her performance status had recovered. The adjuvant course was notable for significant dose modifications and treatment delays:
- Cycle 2: Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) developed, prompting capecitabine dose reduction to 75% (approximately 1,245 mg/m2/day).
- Cycles 3 and 5: Day 8 gemcitabine held due to neutropenia (ANC nadir <1.0 x 10^9/L).
- Cycle 3, day 12: Patient was hospitalized for recurrent peripancreatic fluid collection (4 cm on CT abdomen), with elevated serum amylase and lipase and abdominal pain. This was managed with IV piperacillin-tazobactam, bowel rest, and TPN; no percutaneous drainage was required and the collection resolved within approximately 2 weeks. Chemotherapy was held for 3 weeks during this admission, and capecitabine was further reduced to 50% dose (approximately 830 mg/m2/day) for cycles 4 through 6.

Despite these significant challenges, Ms. Osei-Bonsu completed all 6 planned cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine, albeit over approximately 9 months rather than the planned 6 months. Her most recent CA 19-9 is reported as 18 U/mL, well within the normal range (upper limit of normal 37 U/mL), and represents the nadir since her initial diagnosis. She is presenting today for end-of-adjuvant-therapy review.

Currently, the patient reports mild persistent fatigue, which she attributes to the cumulative toll of surgery and prolonged chemotherapy. She notes mild tingling in her fingertips and toes, which she believes has been stable and is likely residual from prior oxaliplatin exposure during neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX. She denies new abdominal pain, back pain, jaundice, nausea, vomiting, melena, or hematochezia. She reports soft stools consistent with her post-pancreatectomy exocrine insufficiency, currently managed with pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT; pancrelipase 72,000 units lipase per meal). She is checking her blood glucose at home and reports fasting glucose readings in the 110-140 mg/dL range on her current insulin regimen, which she feels is acceptable. No new bone pain, headaches, or respiratory symptoms. She denies any skin changes consistent with hand-foot syndrome at this time (last dose of cape was 3 weeks ago and the redness on her palms has faded). Appetite has improved modestly now that chemotherapy has concluded. She has gained approximately 3 lbs over the past month.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Mild fatigue, improving. Weight gain of 3 lbs over the past month. No fever or chills.
Neurologic: Persistent mild peripheral paresthesias in fingertips and toes (residual from prior oxaliplatin; stable, not worsening).
Gastrointestinal: Loose stools (2-3 per day; consistent with known post-pancreatectomy exocrine insufficiency, well-managed on PERT). No abdominal pain at rest. No nausea or vomiting. No jaundice. No early satiety.
Dermatologic: Resolving hand-foot syndrome; no active erythema or peeling of palms/soles at this visit.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain or joint aches.
Pulmonary: No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
Hematologic: No unusual bruising or bleeding.
Genitourinary: No hematuria. Normal frequency. No dysuria.
Endocrine: Blood glucose under reasonable control on current insulin regimen. Managing well at home with glucose monitoring.
Psychiatric: Patient expresses relief at having completed chemotherapy. Reports some anxiety about disease recurrence and upcoming surveillance imaging. States she is "cautiously optimistic" and engaged with her palliative care team for psychosocial support.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.8°C, weight 62.4 kg (up from nadir of 58 kg during chemoradiation; pre-illness weight approximately 65 kg), height 163 cm, BMI 23.5 kg/m2, SpO2 98% on room air.
ECOG Performance Status: 1.

General: Well-appearing, mildly fatigued West African-descent female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x4. Conversational and engaged.
Head and Neck: No scleral icterus. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary regions bilaterally. No palpable thyroid nodule.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender throughout. Well-healed midline surgical scar from Whipple procedure without hernia or wound dehiscence. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses. No percussion tenderness over the liver edge. No ascites by shifting dullness or fluid wave. Bowel sounds normoactive.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Mild bilateral reduction in light touch sensation over the distal fingertips and toes, consistent with prior documentation of oxaliplatin-related sensory neuropathy. Motor strength intact throughout.
Skin: Palms and soles with mild residual dry skin and faint erythema consistent with resolving grade 1 hand-foot syndrome. No active desquamation. No new rashes.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Coordination intact. Gait steady without ataxia.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
Complete Blood Count:
- WBC 5.2 x 10^9/L (reference 4.5-11.0)
- Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (mildly low; prior nadir 9.8 g/dL; on iron supplementation)
- Platelets 224 x 10^9/L
- ANC 2.8 x 10^9/L (adequate)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL
- ALT 28 U/L, AST 22 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L
- Total protein 6.8 g/dL, Albumin 3.6 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 18 U/mL (normal; upper limit of normal 37 U/mL). This represents the nadir from an initial value of 1,200 U/mL at diagnosis, with subsequent values of 380 U/mL (mid-neoadjuvant), 85 U/mL (post-neoadjuvant, pre-chemoradiation), 52 U/mL (post-chemoradiation, pre-surgery), and 28 U/mL (post-surgical baseline prior to adjuvant therapy). The progressive normalization is reassuring.
- CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (normal; upper limit of normal 3.0 ng/mL).

HbA1c: 7.4% (improved from 9.2% at diagnosis; reflects improved glycemic control post-pancreatectomy on exogenous insulin).

Note: No imaging performed at this visit; patient is due for first post-adjuvant surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate process adenocarcinoma, now having completed her full planned multimodality treatment program including neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX, concurrent long-course chemoradiation with capecitabine, Whipple procedure (pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy, R0, pT3N1M0 Stage IIB), and adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. Despite a complicated postoperative and adjuvant course marked by grade B pancreatic fistula, recurrent peripancreatic fluid collection during chemotherapy, hand-foot syndrome, and myelosuppression requiring dose reductions and delays, she has successfully completed all planned therapy. CA 19-9 and CEA are currently within normal range, and she is clinically without evidence of disease. ECOG performance status is 1.

1. Disease Status — No evidence of recurrence at this time.
   - CA 19-9 18 U/mL and CEA 2.1 ng/mL, both normal, continuing a trend of progressive normalization. This is reassuring but not conclusive, particularly in the context of Stage IIB PDAC with lymph node involvement, perineural invasion, and lymphovascular invasion, all of which confer elevated risk of recurrence.
   - Discussed with the patient that despite R0 resection and completion of all planned therapy, the 2-year recurrence rate for Stage IIB PDAC remains approximately 60-70%, with liver and peritoneal sites being the most common early sites of relapse. She understands this and expressed a desire to remain engaged with close surveillance.
   - Reinforced the importance of surveillance imaging: will plan CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months (first post-adjuvant surveillance scan), then every 3-6 months thereafter for the first 2 years. CA 19-9 and CEA will be drawn at each visit.

2. Peripheral Neuropathy — Stable Grade 1 sensory neuropathy.
   - Residual oxaliplatin-associated peripheral neuropathy remains present but has not worsened, and the patient reports it is not functionally limiting. No further myelotoxic therapy is planned in the immediate setting.
   - Will continue to monitor; duloxetine discussed as a potential option if symptoms become bothersome or worsen over time.

3. Hand-Foot Syndrome — Resolving.
   - Last capecitabine dose was approximately 3 weeks ago; skin on palms and soles showing clinical improvement. No active treatment needed. Patient instructed to continue moisturizing lotion to palms and soles and to avoid prolonged friction or heat exposure to affected areas as they heal fully.

4. Anemia — Mild, likely chemotherapy-related with contribution from iron deficiency.
   - Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL, stable and improving from nadir. Will continue oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg twice daily. Recheck CBC in 6 weeks.
   - No transfusion indicated at this time.

5. Post-Pancreatectomy Exocrine and Endocrine Insufficiency — Managed.
   - Patient on PERT (pancrelipase) at current dose with satisfactory symptom control (2-3 loose stools per day, no steatorrhea complaints). Encouraged to continue taking PERT with all meals and snacks; dose can be titrated upward if steatorrhea recurs.
   - Endocrine insufficiency: HbA1c 7.4%, an improvement from 9.2% at diagnosis. Fasting glucoses 110-140 mg/dL at home. Current insulin regimen (basal-bolus) appears appropriate. Will continue endocrinology co-management.
   - Continued monitoring of fat-soluble vitamin levels (A, D, E, K) and B12 given post-Whipple malabsorption risk; labs to be checked at next visit.

6. Performance Status and Quality of Life — ECOG 1.
   - Patient is managing reasonably well at home and has maintained functional independence throughout treatment. Modest weight recovery noted (62.4 kg vs. nadir of 58 kg). She is retired and lives with family support, which has been critical during the treatment period.
   - Psychosocial support: Palliative care team has been co-managing symptom burden and psychosocial needs throughout the adjuvant period. Encouraged continued engagement with palliative care during surveillance phase.
   - Light physical activity encouraged to combat fatigue and improve glycemic control and overall well-being. Referral to survivorship program offered; patient interested.

7. Medications — Current list reviewed:
   - Metformin 500 mg twice daily (continued for type 2 diabetes; dose maintained given stable renal function)
   - Basal insulin (glargine) plus bolus insulin (lispro) per sliding scale as directed by endocrinology
   - Pancrelipase (pancreatic enzyme replacement) per labeled dose with meals and snacks
   - Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally twice daily
   - Amlodipine 5 mg daily (hypertension)
   - Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly (hyperlipidemia)
   - Ondansetron 8 mg orally as needed for nausea (continue to have on hand)
   - Emollient cream to hands and feet as needed
   - Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed for mild discomfort

8. Follow-up:
   - Next medical oncology appointment in approximately 6-8 weeks for CBC recheck, tumor markers, and clinical assessment.
   - First post-adjuvant surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast scheduled in approximately 3 months.
   - Survivorship clinic referral placed.
   - Patient instructed to contact clinic immediately if she develops new or worsening abdominal pain, jaundice, unintentional weight loss exceeding 5 lbs over 2-3 weeks, or new bone pain, as these would prompt earlier imaging.

Overall, Ms. Osei-Bonsu has made it through a grueling but complete multimodality treatment program and is currently without clinical or biochemical evidence of disease. The fact that she tolerated and completed the full treatment course, despite significant complications, is a testament to her resilience and to the coordinated multidisciplinary care she received. We remain cautiously optimistic about her trajectory.

Time spent with patient in counseling and coordination of care: 45 minutes.
Documented by: [Attending Oncologist, Medical Oncology]
